messages.content:
Here is the conversation transcript between Joe, John and Varun (that is me). The context of the below conversation is as follows:
Context: Non-functional team (NFT) and Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) team need to collaborate as ACoE team want to setup the Centre of Excellence within an organisation. NFT team is responsible for providing various testers to the project team including accessibility testers.

Conversation Transcript:
John: Hi Joe, You was going back up the management chain to get a view on the overarching NFT team covering the gap between the level of assurance provided from a project handed to the CoE.

Joe: We spoke with Struan about the A11y Testers on the most part being a pool based out of NFT. I think we have agreement in principle. We did agree that most agile squads would need an A11y Tester permanently embedded in a squad - this would be required to truly shift left on RLGs A11y compliance as a whole. I believe it will become clearer with time just how many A11y Testers will be required. I suspect you guys are probably a bit optimistic on the number and the ACoE is pessimistic on the number (i.e. need too many) - let's not worry about the specifics of that at the moment.
Varun and I are still actively building out the Roadmap for ACoE and have made good headway. We will come back to you once we've got that and can share plans - Next week is my guess.

John: Embedding a specific Accessibility tester into each squad might not be the right approach. We are just ready to roll out a guide to what the performance team service can offer which can be used for Accessibility. Manage - Build, run and maintain the performance test suite, Support - Help build the test suite, provide guidance to the team/project on how to take things forward, Consult - the team/project are skilled up to build, run and maintain their performance suite. If a squad brings in a skilled accessibility tester to build, run and maintain their test suite. Once complete, they will have an expensive resource, at best, doing a side role when there is not accessibility testing to do. What the NFT team is offering is to engage with the team, provide hands on when needed, support through the process of becoming compliant and providing training/upskilling to whoever wants to learn on an ongoing basis.

Joe: I've asked Varun Padiyar if he is able to work this through. Varun, I think it would be helpful if we could have visibility of the guide John is talking about. Can you please send this to us John White? One note before I let Varun continue ... I dare say that you may be over simplifying by saying "Build, run and maintain" the suite. Yes, automation sort of runs itself once it is set up, and people are paying attention to the results. About 50% of Accessibility testing is manual testing and will be ongoing throughout the life of a product. We need to set ourselves up so that each agile squad has the ability to carry out necessary manual A11y testing for each of their work items in their sprints. Otherwise they will try to release a product that has not been fully A11y tested.

John:
Hi Joe, when I say maintain, I'm talking about the whole process. For each manual test, there should be a script that is followed so the test is repeatable, there should be knowledge transfer and upskilling, and anything that is automated there should a maintenance wrapper around it. The whole test piece in this area should have the relevant attention paid to it as you would expect with anything as critical, carrying such a potential impact on credibility, as Accessibility. I think we might be getting our wires crossed here. Also, we need to make sure that change which falls into a waterfall'ish type of bucket is also covered.

Varun: Hi John, thanks for sharing your thoughts. It’s great to have your expertise as we figure out how to best integrate non-functional testing services within our teams and ACoE.
I wanted to touch on why we might really benefit from having someone focus just on accessibility within each team:

Accessibility is specialised and having someone dedicated means we’re always on the ball with the latest guidelines.
With a dedicated tester, we can make sure accessibility gets the continuous attention it needs, which is crucial.
When we’re working quickly in Agile, having an expert around means we can sort any issues out straight away.
If someone’s trying to handle both accessibility and performance testing, they might not be able to give each area enough attention. Accessibility needs a full-time eye.
A tester who knows accessibility inside out will always keep our users’ needs at the forefront, making sure our products are inclusive.
Now, regarding the Waterfall projects, I fully recognise that their structure is more linear than Agile. However, the benefits of having a dedicated accessibility tester are just as important, if not more so. Waterfall projects can sometimes silo different phases such as design, development, and testing. A dedicated accessibility tester ensures that at each stage, accessibility requirements are clearly defined and met before moving to the next phase. This minimises the risk of discovering major accessibility issues late in the project when they are more costly and time-consuming to fix.
Having someone consistently championing accessibility throughout the Waterfall process embeds it into the project from the very beginning. This helps prevent any last-minute surprises that could delay project timelines and ensures a high-quality product for all users.
Let’s keep working together to create a process that supports both Agile and Waterfall methods, helping RLG lead the way in inclusive design.
John: Cheers for the above Varun, much appreciated. We might be tackling this in the wrong way. We shouldn't be thinking of working in a waterfall or agile way, Accessibility needs to be embedded in the work to be done from the start and, as we agreed, that needs the right lens applying. Embedding 'Accessibility' from the earliest point is key.

Varun: Yes John, completely agree with your thought. We always recommend that accessibility should be included right from the "ideation" phase.

John: The framework looks to put the onerous on project to get to a point in time where they have completed their assurance work, then sending this to the CoE to validate. This has a potential to create a bottle neck.Aside from that, embedding an Accessibility SME tester to each team/squad/project would not only be very costly but inefficient. A change could have no impact on Accessibility. The expensive SME would either be sat doing nothing or providing support for work that is below their pay grade. I'm not saying that an SME shouldn't be budgeted for, I'm saying it needs to be thought about and cost effective, which is something I've done on the performance area of NFT.

John: On Tuesday. myself and Varun had a conversation around if there was a need for the NFT team to provide any support in the process that change goes through on it's path to live and ticking all the boxes for Accessibility. Points captured from the meeting on Tuesday: -

Agreed that there is a skills gap at the moment with the understanding of Accessibility that teams and projects have, and what needs to be done to be compliant
This has the potential to impact project go live
Change being delivered in both agile and waterfall methodologies need to be compliant, they both need to be thought about and engage in the process with the ACoE
The hand over process and impact needs to be worked through
When will a project have to complete their Accessibility artefacts and provide them to the ACoE?
If this point is a large amount of time before go live this will impact agile projects
If this is small amount of time before go live, if major issues are encountered that require a lot of effort and time to fix, this will impact go live
Having a number of Accessibility SMEs in a small team, responsible for NFT, would significantly mitigated the above risks
To make this work, who ever is delivering this change should make appropriate adjustments to their budget at the relevant phase
The next step is to work out the engagement process that all parties should follow. This will identify any touch points where there might be friction. Varun, please add to this if you think I have missed anything.
Varun: Thanks John. These basically sums up our conversation. I would also like to add some additional points which can be useful:

Regarding the responsibility for ensuring accessibility implementation, it is important to clarify that while the ACoE governs accessibility in the organization, the ultimate responsibility lies with the project team. The ACoE is there to provide guidance, support, and ensure compliance, but it is the project team’s responsibility to implement accessibility correctly.
If at any point it becomes evident that the project go-live is being impacted by the ACoE process , the project team has the option to raise a deferment request. This request would allow them to proceed with the go-live while ensuring that any accessibility issues are addressed promptly in a future release.
Your task is to analyze the above conversation thoroughly and provide an engagement process that both ACoE and NFT team can follow.

Based on the conversation transcript provided, it's clear that there's a need for clear communication and role clarity between the Non-functional team (NFT) and the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE). Here is an engagement process that should help address the concerns raised and allow both teams to effectively collaborate:

1. **Integration and Communication Plan:**
   - Schedule regular joint meetings for the ACoE and NFT teams to discuss upcoming projects, resource needs, and progress on current initiatives.
   - Establish communication channels (e.g., email, chat groups, etc.) to facilitate quick consultations and knowledge sharing.

2. **Resource Management and Allocation:**
   - Develop a flexible staffing model that can scale up or down based on project demands, ensuring that there are enough Accessibility SMEs for different teams while managing costs.
   - Create an internal pool of trained accessibility testers within the NFT that can be temporarily embedded into Agile squads when needed.

3. **Knowledge Sharing and Training:**
   - ACoE to provide regular training and workshops to ensure that project teams and Agile squads are up to date with the latest accessibility guidelines and compliance requirements.
   - Set up a knowledge repository with materials that team members can access for guidance on accessibility-related queries.

4. **Embedding Accessibility from Start:**
   - Accessibility to be included in the project's ideation phase to ensure that it's part of the product lifecycle and not an afterthought.
   - Provide projects with checklists and frameworks to ensure accessibility considerations are factored in from the beginning.

5. **Ensuring Efficient Use of Resources:**
   - Instead of permanently assigning an Accessibility SME to each team, control costs by having SMEs work with multiple squads and consult as needed.
   - Use an 'Accessibility Champion' model where certain individuals in the squad are upskilled to carry out routine accessibility checks under the guidance of the SME.

6. **Project Lifecycle Process Adaptation:**
   - Develop a process that includes accessibility checks at key points in the project lifecycle for both Agile and Waterfall methodologies.
   - For Agile projects, ensure these checks are part of the sprint activities. For Waterfall projects, ensure they are part of phase gates or milestones.

7. **Assessment and Compliance Tracking:**
   - Standardize the handover process to the ACoE for accessibility validation, outlining when and what artefacts need to be provided.
   - Implement a review process whereby the ACoE can assess compliance at agreed-upon stages before final validation and approval.

8. **Risk Management and Mitigation:**
   - Create protocols for identifying and raising accessibility risks early, allowing for timely and cost-effective resolutions.
   - Develop a process for deferment requests, where projects that are non-compliant can go live with a concrete plan to rectify issues in a subsequent release.

9. **Budget and Cost Consideration:**
   - Integrate accessibility cost considerations into project budgets early to avoid late-stage financial surprises.
   - Track and report on the ROI of accessibility efforts to reinforce the value proposition of investing in accessibility.

10. **Process Documentation and Continuous Improvement:**
    - Document the agreed-upon engagement process and make it accessible to all stakeholders.
    - Establish a feedback loop where both ACoE and NFT teams can suggest improvements to the process based on their experiences.

By following this engagement process, the ACoE and NFT teams can work synergistically, ensuring that accessibility is a well-integrated component of product development, resources are utilized efficiently, and compliance is achieved without unnecessary cost overruns or project delays.

This request would allow them to proceed with the go-live while ensuring that any accessibility issues are addressed promptly in a future release.

Your task is to analyze the above conversation thoroughly and provide an engagement process that both ACoE and NFT team can follow. This should identify touch points where there might be friction.

Based on the conversation transcript and the task given, the following engagement process could help ACoE and NFT to collaborate effectively while minimizing friction:

### Engagement Process for ACoE and NFT Collaboration

#### 1. Initial Assessment & Planning Phase
   - **Integration Meeting:** Hold initial meetings between ACoE and NFT leads to align priorities and expectations.
   - **Resource Assessment:** Determine the number of Accessibility SMEs needed based on project forecasts and decide on a blended approach for embedding A11y Testers into teams or having pool-based resources.
   - **Roadmap Sharing:** ACoE shares its tentative Roadmap with NFT to ensure alignment and to identify the touchpoints for collaboration.

#### 2. Knowledge Transfer & Training
   - **Accessibility Guides:** NFT shares guides and best practices for accessibility testing with ACoE and project teams.
   - **Training Programs:** ACoE provides training sessions for NFT on accessibility guidelines and manual testing procedures to upskill testers.
   - **Skill Gap Analysis:** Conduct workshops to identify and address the existing skills gap in understanding and implementing accessibility.

#### 3. Process Integrations
   - **Incorporation of Accessibility:** Ensure that accessibility is included right from the ideation phase of the project.
   - **Testing Protocols:** Develop clear protocols and scripts for manual and automated accessibility testing that Agile squads can follow.

#### 4. Resource Engagement & Support
   - **Dedicated or Shared SMEs:** Depending on project requirements, decide when a dedicated Accessibility SME is necessary for a squad or when shared support is sufficient.
   - **Support Mechanism:** NFT provides ongoing support and engagement through the process of becoming compliant with accessibility standards.

#### 5. Continuous Compliance & Assurance
   - **Compliance Validation:** Establish checkpoints for ACoE to review accessibility artefacts and validate compliance at predetermined project stages.
   - **Deferment Process:** Create a process for teams to raise deferment requests if compliance might impact go-live schedules.

#### 6. Coordination of Methodologies
   - **Agile & Waterfall Considerations:** Tailor accessibility engagement to fit both Agile and Waterfall methodologies, ensuring compliance is maintained throughout different project management styles.
   - **Documentation & Artefacts:** Standardize document formats and artefact requirements for easier integration and validation.

#### 7. Budget Planning & Management
   - **Budget Adjustments:** Integrate accessibility costs in project budgets and plan for SME engagement to be cost-effective across the project lifecycle.
   - **Efficiency Tracking:** Analyze the cost versus benefits of embedding Accessibility SMEs across projects.

#### 8. Risk Management & Mitigation
   - **Risk Identification:** Have regular sync-up meetings to identify potential risks related to accessibility early in the project.
   - **Risk Mitigation Strategies:** Prepare contingency plans for addressing accessibility issues that could impact project timelines.

#### 9. Feedback Loop & Improvement
   - **Feedback Sessions:** Conduct post-project reviews and retrospectives to identify what worked well and what didn't, then adjust the collaboration process accordingly.
   - **Process Evolution:** Regularly update the engagement process based on shared experiences to reduce friction and improve efficiency.

#### 10. Reporting & Metrics
   - **Compliance Reporting:** Provide regular updates on accessibility testing results and compliance status to all stakeholders.
   - **Performance Metrics:** Track and analyze the performance of the integrated team in terms of meeting accessibility standards, efficiency, and cost.

#### 11. Communication Strategy
   - **Regular Check-Ins:** Schedule frequent check-in meetings to discuss progress and sort out any issues.
   - **Central Communication Platform:** Use a central platform for communication and documentation to keep all parties informed and engaged.

By following this engagement process, the ACoE and NFT can effectively navigate the complexities of integrating accessibility testing services within the teams and projects. The process aims to recognize and respond to potential friction points, such as skill gaps, resource allocation, and methodology differences, while emphasizing the importance of building accessibility considerations into the product lifecycle from the outset.
Here, the NFT team doesn't know anything about accessibility. The accessibility expertise is within ACoE team only. Based on this information, refined your engagement process now.

Apologies for the previous oversight. With the clarified context that accessibility expertise lies solely within the ACoE team and not within the NFT, here is a refined engagement process:

### Refined Engagement Process for ACoE and NFT Collaboration

#### 1. Initial Alignment & Integration
   - **Kick-off Workshop:** Conduct an initial workshop between ACoE and the NFT to establish common understanding, set expectations, and align on the role of ACoE as the sole provider of accessibility expertise.
   - **Integration Plan Development:** Create a plan for how ACoE will support project teams, and how the NFT will facilitate this integration without assuming deep accessibility knowledge.

#### 2. ACoE-Led Training & Knowledge Transfer
   - **Basics of Accessibility:** ACoE develops and delivers training sessions for the NFT and project teams covering the basics of accessibility, relevant laws, and standards.
   - **Awareness Programs:** Roll out accessibility awareness programs across the organization, facilitated by ACoE, to highlight the importance and implications of accessibility.

#### 3. Clear Definition of Roles
   - **Clarify Roles:** Define the specific roles and responsibilities of ACoE as accessibility experts and establish how the NFT will support without overlapping functionalities.
   - **Support Functions:** ACoE determines the type of ongoing support it will provide to project teams, including consulting, advising, and checking compliance.

#### 4. Ongoing ACoE Support & Engagement
   - **Accessibility Consultation:** ACoE offers dedicated consultation hours for project teams to seek guidance on integrating accessibility into their workflows.
   - **Compliance Reviews:** ACoE performs periodic compliance reviews at key milestones for projects.

#### 5. Accessibility Integration in Development Lifecycle
   - **Inclusive Ideation:** ACoE provides guidelines to project teams for embedding accessibility from the ideation phase.
   - **Accessibility Checklists:** ACoE develops and distributes checklists to ensure project teams incorporate accessibility consistently at various phases.

#### 6. ACoE-Led Validation & Verification Process
   - **Artefact Creation:** ACoE develops templates and examples for the necessary accessibility artefacts, such as testing scripts and compliance reports.
   - **Validation Points:** Establish regular checkpoints where ACoE validates the accessibility work done by the project teams.

#### 7. Resource Planning & Allocation
   - **Proper Forecasting:** Collaborate on resource allocation based on the projected needs and complexity of upcoming projects.
   - **Cost-Effective Solutions:** Devise cost-effective approaches for ACoE support, possibly through a shared resource model where the ACoE offers guidance to several projects in a structured manner.

#### 8. Workflow for Waterfall and Agile Projects
   - **Agile and Waterfall Documentation:** ACoE provides clear guidelines for integrating accessibility within both methodologies.
   - **Adjusted Timelines:** Work with project teams to ensure that accessibility artefacts are developed in time for ACoE review without impacting project schedules.

#### 9. Escalation & Deferment Protocol
   - **Risk Escalation Path:** Set an escalation path for accessibility issues that threaten to impact project delivery.
   - **Deferment Requests:** Formalize the process for project teams to request deferments when faced with accessibility issues that could delay go-live.

#### 10. Process Documentation & Continuous Refinement
   - **Document Process:** Ensure there is thorough documentation of the engagement process, roles, responsibilities, and resources.
   - **Iterative Improvements:** Establish a regular review cycle to assess the effectiveness of the process and make necessary adjustments.

#### 11. Communication & Reporting Structure
   - **Regular Communication:** Establish regular check-ins and updates between ACoE, the NFT, and project teams to discuss developments and address issues.
   - **Dashboard Metrics:** ACoE creates dashboards or reports that track accessibility milestones, issues, and the outcomes of consultations, making them accessible to all stakeholders.

By adapting this engagement process, the relationship between the ACoE and NFT is clarified, enabling the ACoE to take a proactive lead in infusing accessibility into organizational projects with the support of the NFT, while remaining cost-effective and efficient. The roles of each team are clearly defined, and the ACoE has the necessary structures in place to impart their specialized knowledge to the broader organization.

The ACoE team is not responsible for providing the accessibility associate. That task remains with the NFT team. The NFT team will provide the accessibility associate. Based on this information, refine your engagement process.

Given this updated context, the ACoE team will guide and set standards for accessibility, while the NFT team will provide the actual accessibility resources (associates/testers) for the projects. Let's refine the engagement process accordingly:

### Refined Engagement Process for Collaboration

#### 1. Knowledge and Standards Sharing
   - **Training and Certification:** The ACoE develops a training and certification program that NFT associates must complete before they are deployed on projects.
   - **Standard Guidelines:** The ACoE crafts comprehensive accessibility guidelines and checklists which the NFT will use in their testing activities.

#### 2. Accessibility Resource Provisioning
   - **Resource Pool Establishment:** The NFT creates and manages a pool of accessibility associates who have been trained by the ACoE.
   - **Project Allocation:** The NFT is responsible for assigning accessibility associates to projects based on their expertise and the project needs, following a consultation with ACoE on the complexity of accessibility requirements.

#### 3. Inclusive Development Lifecycle Integration
   - **Early Engagement:** Accessibility associates from the NFT are brought in early during the development lifecycle to promote "shift left" on accessibility.
   - **Accessibility Evangelism:** NFT associates act as champions of accessibility in their project teams, promoting best practices and standards set forth by the ACoE.

#### 4. Ongoing Support and Consultation
   - **ACoE Consultation:** ACoE avails itself to the NFT and project teams for advanced accessibility queries and for periodic reviews of project accessibility health.
   - **Support Model:** The ACoE establishes a support model for NFT accessibility associates, which might include office hours, discussion forums, and regular update meetings.

#### 5. Process and Compliance Reviews
   - **Artefact Review:** The ACoE sets up regular intervals to review and validate accessibility artefacts and compliance created by NFT accessibility associates within projects.
   - **Bottleneck Management:** Procedures are set to prevent and manage any bottlenecks in the validation process to ensure smooth project flow and deadline adherence.

#### 6. Cross-Methodology Accommodation
   - **Methodology Guidelines:** The ACoE provides separate sets of instructions tailored for implementing accessibility in Agile, Waterfall, and hybrid methodologies.
   - **Milestone Integration:** The ACoE helps define the milestones at which accessibility checks must be made during different project phases.

#### 7. Efficient Resource Utilization and Budgeting
   - **Resource Forecasting:** The NFT works with project teams to forecast when and how many accessibility associates are needed throughout the project life to optimize costs.
   - **Budget Collaboration:** The ACoE assists with identifying accessibility-related costs and projections, helping project teams factor these into their budgets.

#### 8. Risk Identification and Mitigation Planning
   - **Risk Management:** The ACoE helps the NFT identify potential accessibility risks and plan appropriate mitigation strategies.
   - **Deferment Strategy:** A framework is created for managing deferment requests when accessibility issues are detected close to project go-live dates.

#### 9. Continuous Process Evaluation and Adjustment
   - **Feedback Loop:** There is an established feedback mechanism between NFT testers, the ACoE, and project teams to discuss what's working and what areas need improvement.
   - **Process Development:** A process for reviewing and updating accessibility testing methodologies and practices is put in place to remain up-to-date with industry standards.

#### 10. Reporting and Performance Monitoring
   - **Tracking Metrics:** The ACoE creates clear metrics for tracking the effectiveness of the accessibility process and outcomes of accessibility testing.
   - **Performance Reviews:** Regular reporting and performance review meetings are held to ensure that the accessibility goals are being achieved effectively.

#### 11. Team Communication Framework
   - **Regular Sync-Ups:** Regular meetings between ACoE and NFT representatives are organized to keep both teams in sync and address any emerging challenges.
   - **Documented Communication Channels:** All communication regarding accessibility requirements, changes, or updates are documented and shared across teams in a centralized manner.

By following this engagement process, the ACoE will set the stage for proper accessibility protocols while the NFT will operationalize these protocols by providing trained accessibility associates to work within the project teams, thus fostering a collaborative and efficient environment for implementing accessibility standards across the organization.

We don't want NFT team exploits any kind loophole with the engagement process. Can you please check this once again and let me know whether there is any loophole NFT may utilize in order to not provide accessibility resources?

To minimize the potential for any loopholes that the NFT team might exploit to avoid providing accessibility resources, the engagement process should have clear, verifiable steps with specific deliverables, well-defined timelines, and accountability measures. Let's outline the points to address this:

1. **Clear Mandate and Policy Enforcement:** There should be a top-down mandate that reinforces the importance of accessibility and the requirement for NFT to provide qualified accessibility associates to projects. This mandate should be backed by policy with executive support, making the provision of accessibility resources non-negotiable.

2. **Training Completion Verification:** Completion of the ACoE-led training program should be mandatory for all NFT accessibility associates. Records of their certification and training should be auditable, providing evidence that associates are qualified before they are deployed on projects.

3. **Defined Resource Allocation Plan:** The NFT should be required to provide a documented resource allocation plan that transparently shows how accessibility resources will be distributed across projects. This plan should be reviewed regularly and updated as necessary.

4. **Project Accountability:** Project managers should be held accountable for ensuring their project teams include the mandated NFT-provided accessibility resources. Failure to do so should trigger a review by the ACoE to identify the reasons behind the omission and to take corrective action.

5. **Regular Compliance Audits:** Implement frequent, unannounced audits carried out by the ACoE or third-party auditors to ensure that NFT is adhering to the agreed provision of accessibility resources.

6. **Escalation Protocol:** Establish a clear escalation process for projects that do not receive the necessary accessibility resources from the NFT. The protocol should guide teams on what to do and whom to contact if accessibility resource objectives are not met.

7. **Performance Metrics and Reporting:** Develop key performance indicators (KPIs) to measure the NFT's success in providing accessibility resources. Regular reporting against these KPIs should be required, and these reports should be made accessible to stakeholders at multiple levels of the organization.

8. **Consequences for Non-compliance:** Define and communicate the repercussions for the NFT team if they consistently fail to provide the necessary accessibility associates. This can range from internal reviews to resource reallocation or even budgetary penalties.

9. **Feedback and Issue Resolution:** Build a formalized feedback system where any team can report issues related to the provisioning of accessibility resources by NFT. These issues should trigger a transparent review and resolution process.

10. **Transparency in Resourcing Decisions:** All decisions about the allocation of resources and any changes to the engagement process should be documented and communicated to all stakeholders.

11. **Oversight Committee:** Establish an oversight committee with representatives from ACoE, NFT, and other relevant stakeholders. This committee will meet at regular intervals to review compliance with the engagement process and address any identified loopholes proactively.

By incorporating these steps into the engagement process, any potential loopholes that could enable the NFT to avoid providing necessary accessibility resources should be closed. Each step introduces checks, balances, and transparency, making it difficult for NFT to circumvent the agreed-upon procedures for supporting accessibility across the organization's projects.
Now based on above steps you provided, craft a final version of the engagement process for ACoE and NFT team.

Here is the final version of the engagement process that incorporates robust measures designed to ensure the Non-functional team (NFT) meets its obligation to provide accessibility resources as per the agreement with the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE):

### Final Engagement Process for ACoE and NFT Collaboration

#### 1. Establish Organization-wide Accessibility Mandate
   - An executive-driven mandate reinforces the critical importance of accessibility.
   - Policies are established to back up the provision of NFT accessibility resources with clearly defined consequences for non-compliance.

#### 2. Accessibility Training and Certification
   - Accessibility associates from NFT must complete ACoE-led training and certification.
   - Training completion is auditable, and only certified associates are deployed on projects.

#### 3. Documented Resource Allocation and Planning
   - NFT provides a transparent resource allocation plan, which is updated regularly and reviewed by ACoE.
   - The plan ensures equitable and adequate distribution of accessibility resources among projects.

#### 4. Project Team Accountability
   - Project managers are held responsible for incorporating NFT-provided accessibility associates into their teams.
   - Non-compliance initiates an ACoE review to identify and rectify the issue.

#### 5. Regular Compliance Audits
   - Periodic, unannounced audits by the ACoE or independent auditors verify NFT compliance with resource provisioning.
   - Audit results are documented and shared with the oversight committee.

#### 6. Escalation and Resolution Protocols
   - A clear escalation process is in place for non-receipt of accessibility resources, providing guidance for project teams on whom to inform.
   - The oversight committee is involved in resolving escalated issues.

#### 7. Key Performance Metrics and Transparent Reporting
   - NFT's success in providing accessibility resources is measured through KPIs.
   - Regular reporting against these KPIs is mandatory, with results accessible to stakeholders.

#### 8. Define Repercussions for Non-compliance
   - Consequences of the NFT's failure to meet its obligations are outlined and include reviews, resource reallocation, or budgetary implications.
   - Repercussions are communicated to all NFT members to ensure awareness.

#### 9. Formal Feedback System
   - Teams can formally report issues with accessibility resource provisioning from NFT.
   - Reported issues trigger a transparent review process handled by the oversight committee.

#### 10. Transparent Resourcing Decisions
   - All decisions regarding the allocation of NFT accessibility resources and process changes are well-documented.
   - Communications about resource decisions are openly shared with all parties involved.

#### 11. Establish an Oversight Committee
   - A committee including members from ACoE, NFT, and stakeholder representatives reviews resource provisioning compliance.
   - The committee meets regularly to address potential loopholes and enforce the engagement process.

By following this structured and transparent engagement process, NFT must adhere to its commitment to providing accessibility resources, ensuring ongoing compliance, and supporting the overall goal of universal accessibility within the organization. The ACoE takes on a supervisory role to uphold quality and standards, while the NFT becomes a reliable partner in delivering accessible digital assets.
This engagement process is complicated and might not be clear with the NFT team. I want you to create a simplified version which tells the step-by-step process ACoE team and NFT team will follow.

Certainly, let's simplify the process into a clear, step-by-step guide. The following is an engagement process that ACoE team and NFT team will follow:

### Simplified Engagement Process for ACoE and NFT Collaboration

#### Step 1: Training and Certification
- **NFT**: Send team members to ACoE-led training.
- **AcoE**: Provide accessibility training and keep records of who is certified.

#### Step 2: Resource Planning
- **NFT**: Create a clear schedule for providing accessibility testers to projects. Share this schedule with ACoE.
- **AcoE**: Review and approve the resource plan.

#### Step 3: Project Integration
- **Project Managers**: Ensure an NFT-provided accessibility tester is part of your team from the start.
- **NFT Accessibility Testers**: Begin work early in the project and follow ACoE's guidelines.

#### Step 4: Compliance Checks
- **AcoE**: Randomly audit projects to check for the presence and effective work of accessibility testers.
- **NFT**: Cooperation with audits and make any required improvements.

#### Step 5: Escalation Procedure
- **Project Teams**: If an NFT accessibility tester is not provided, raise the issue immediately using the established escalation path.
- **AcoE**: Address escalated issues and take corrective actions as needed.

#### Step 6: Regular Reporting
- **NFT**: Report regularly on the provision of accessibility testers and any challenges.
- **AcoE**: Monitor reports and provide feedback.

#### Step 7: Performance Review
- **AcoE**: Lead performance reviews with input from all stakeholders to ensure the process is working and improving.

By following these seven simplified steps, the ACoE and NFT can work together effectively, ensuring that accessibility is integrated from the inception of every project and maintained throughout, with clear accountability and paths for reporting and escalation as needed.

Analyze the WCAG with their suggestions.

Let's analyze each of the provided Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) success criteria and consider the associated suggestions:

### WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast
**Criterion**: The visual presentation of user interface components and graphical objects must have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors to be perceptible by individuals with moderately low vision or color deficiencies.

**Suggestions**:
- **2px Focus Indicator with 3:1 Contrast**: Adding a 2px focus indicator around interactive elements (like buttons and form fields) is a practical way to achieve the required contrast ratio. This visual enhancement helps users with visual impairments to recognize the active element they are interacting with.
  
- **2 Color Focus Indicator (e.g. black & white)**: Implementing a focus indicator that uses two contrasting colors can help ensure that it stands out irrespective of the background color. This approach aims to maximize visibility and address situations where changing background colors could render a single-color focus indicator less distinguishable.

### WCAG 2.4.2 Page Titled
**Criterion**: Web pages should have titles that are descriptive of the content or function of the page, assisting users in understanding the context and guiding them effectively.

**Suggestions**:
- **Use of “:” or “-“ in Titles**: The recommendation to use colons (:) or hyphens (-) instead of the pipeline (|) symbol aims to maintain a consistent and clear visual separation between segments of a page title, which is particularly helpful for screen reader users. This consistency in punctuation can be beneficial for users who rely on assistive technology to better understand the structure and hierarchy of the information.

### WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible
**Criterion**: It is necessary that when interfacing with a website through a keyboard, users can visually determine the focused element. This ensures that users who do not use a mouse can still navigate and interact with the website.

**Suggestions**:
- **2 Color Focus Indicator (e.g. black & white)**: As with WCAG 1.4.11, providing a two-color focus indicator enhances visibility for users as they navigate using the keyboard. 

- **Using CSS Techniques for Visible Focus**: While browsers generally provide a default focus style, it often does not meet the visibility requirements of WCAG. Tailoring focus styles using CSS ensures a consistent and pleasant experience across different browsers and goes beyond the minimum accessibility standards to enhance usability.

In summary, the suggestions provided for each of the WCAG success criteria aim to enhance the accessibility and usability for individuals with visual impairments or those using keyboard navigation. The focus on contrasts, visibility, and descriptive labels aligns with the core principles of WCAG to create content that is perceivable, operable, and understandable for all users, regardless of their abilities or the devices they use to access the web.
Are the suggestions correct or any changes need to be done?

The suggestions provided are generally in line with the intent of the WCAG success criteria, aiming to improve accessibility. However, there are some points to consider for clarification or correction to ensure the suggestions align precisely with best practices:

### WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast

- **2px Focus Indicator with 3:1 Contrast**: This suggestion is good practice; however, it specifically addresses WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible) more than WCAG 1.4.11. WCAG 1.4.11 is about the contrast of visual elements such as buttons, icons, and other user interface components against their background. While a 2px focus indicator will help with focus visibility, it does not necessarily address all aspects of non-text contrast.
  
- **2 Color Focus Indicator (e.g. black & white)**: Using two colors for a focus indicator is indeed beneficial, but it is essential to ensure that either color has a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against the adjacent colors. Also, "weight" is not a color, so it might be a typo or misunderstanding—it should potentially be "white" or another color that ensures sufficient contrast.

### WCAG 2.4.2 Page Titled

- **Use of “:” or “-“ in Titles**: The recommendation is partially correct. While consistency in punctuation can be helpful for users, there is no WCAG requirement that specifies which symbols to use in page titles. The key point is the title's description of the page content or purpose, regardless of symbols used for separation. Therefore, while suggesting consistency is good, stating that the pipeline (|) symbol is "not allowed" is not accurate per WCAG standards.

### WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible

- **2 Color Focus Indicator (e.g. black & white)**: This suggestion also appeared in WCAG 1.4.11 and again needs clarification regarding the use of "weight" instead of "white." It also needs to ensure that the focus indicator has sufficient contrast compliance as per WCAG 1.4.11's requirements when applied to the visual focus indication.

- **Using CSS Techniques for Visible Focus**: This is a valid suggestion. Customizing focus indicators with CSS is an excellent way to ensure visibility across browsers and conform to WCAG standards. Developers should ensure that custom focus indicators are at least as visible as the browser’s default focus indicator and comply with the 3:1 contrast ratio.

In conclusion, the suggestions are on the right track but may need minor adjustments for clarity and accuracy. Specifically, for WCAG 1.4.11, clarifying that the focus indicator should not only be thick but also high contrast, and for WCAG 2.4.2, removing the incorrect information about prohibited symbols. By doing so, the recommendations will provide clearer and more precise guidance for improving accessibility.
How would you correct the suggestions? Provide me correct suggestion on the WCAG I provided.

Certainly! Here are the revised and validated suggestions for each of the WCAG success criteria:

### WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast

**Criterion**: The visual presentation of user interface components and graphical objects must have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors to be easily seen by users with visual impairments.

**Revised Suggestions**:
1. **Focus Indicator**: Provide a visible focus indicator with a minimum thickness of 2px for interactive components. Ensure that the focus indicator color achieves a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against the component's background to enhance visibility for users navigating via keyboard.

2. **Use High Contrast Colors**: Implement high contrast colors for interactive elements and their focus indicators to cater to users with color vision deficiencies. Examples include using black and white or other color combinations that easily stand out from the component's immediate background and guarantee at least a 3:1 contrast ratio.

### WCAG 2.4.2 Page Titled

**Criterion**: Web pages should have titles that clearly and concisely describe the content or purpose, enabling users to understand the context and navigate effectively.

**Revised Suggestions**:
1. **Descriptive Titles**: Ensure web pages have descriptive titles that reflect the page's content or purpose. It's acceptable to use colons (:), hyphens (-), or pipes (|) to separate different elements of the title, like the page name and the organization name. Consistency across the website in the symbols' usage is key to helping users predict and understand the titles.

### WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible

**Criterion**: It is essential that when interfacing with a website via keyboard, the keyboard focus is clearly and consistently visible.

**Revised Suggestions**:
1. **High Contrast Focus Indicator**: Provide a distinct focus indicator that contrasts well with both the background and the element receiving focus to ensure it's easily visible. If using a 2px outline, ensure it has a contrast ratio of at least 3:1.

2. **Custom CSS for Focus Styles**: While browsers may offer a default indication of focus, augment this with custom CSS styles to create a focus style that is clear and recognizable across all browsers and better supports users who rely on keyboard navigation.

In these adjustments, we have clarified the WCAG requirements for non-text contrast to include focus indicators and emphasized the importance of consistency and clarity in page titles without prohibiting specific symbols. For focus visibility, the suggestions now specify the need for custom CSS that offers improved keyboard navigation visibility across different browsers.
